source: Destination Trail medical director email — Tahoe 200 Full Loop (May 2026)

sleep_guidance.start_by: Night 2 — ideally within 24–40 hours of the start
sleep_guidance.nap_benefit: Even 30–60 minutes helps a lot for safety and your head
sleep_guidance.pull_risk: Bad delusion or unsafe decisions can mean a pull — talk to staff if you're struggling

sections.title: General safety
bullets: Temperature swings are real — hot, cold, wind, dust, all of it.
Fix problems at an aid before you leave.
Sleep, food, water, and dry clothes solve most problems.
Carry more layers and calories than you think you need.
Fix things early; don't wait until you're desperate.

sections.title: Helping other runners
bullets: If someone asks for help, help if you can.
For a rescue: get bib number (photo helps), location, and what's wrong.
Don't wake runners sleeping safely off-trail.
If someone is on or right next to the trail, check on them.

sections.title: Heat
bullets: Watch for nausea, confusion, red/dry skin, or not sweating.
Shade, cool slowly, wet clothes and skin, sip fluids, salty food.
If you start shivering while cooling, ease off cooling and warm up a bit.

sections.title: When to call for help
bullets: Unless it's immediately life-threatening, contact race staff before 911 — they often reach you faster.
Most rescues mean walking out, even with med support.
Helicopters are for extreme, life-threatening cases only.
Wait times can be 4–24 hours; weather and terrain slow everything.
Costs outside what Destination Trail runs are on the runner.

sections.title: Cold and hypothermia
bullets: Carry the mandatory warm/waterproof kit — you'll need it.
Conditions flip fast with elevation and time of day.
If you're hypothermic: layers on, calories in, keep moving if it's safe.

sections.title: Dust and Rubicon cough
bullets: Full loop goes through the Rubicon — miles of fine powder dust.
Bring a reusable mask, disposables, and a wet buff.
Try face coverings on training runs; some feel awful on climbs.
Mucinex and similar can help — test before race day.

sections.title: If you're lost
bullets: GPX on a charged device, stay on trail — no shortcuts.
If you're really lost: stop, stay put, save battery, wait for help.

sections.title: Sleep and hallucinations
bullets: Sleep debt causes bad decisions, hallucinations, and unsafe behavior.
Start sleeping by Night 2 (ideally within 24–40 hours of start).
Tell staff if your head or body isn't right.

sections.title: Feet
bullets: Foot care is on me — blisters and wet feet early, not at mile 180.
Hot spots now; waiting for the next aid is a bad bet over 200 miles.
Let feet dry when I'm eating or sleeping at aids — it pays off later.
Gaiters — the dust on this course is brutal without them.
